title: The Prophet from the Marsh
paragraphs:
The rain had not stopped for thirty-one days.

Not the honest rain of summer that came in a single long breath and left the earth smelling like broken iron. This rain was different. It fell in threads, slow and patient, and it smelled faintly of something sweet going bad. The people of Aldmoor stopped bringing in the washing after the second week. Cloth left on the line came back stained the color of weak tea, and no amount of boiling would lift it out.

The village priest, Father Aulen, stood on the chapel steps and watched the water pool in the graveyard. The oldest stones had begun to tilt. Worms, shining and pale as fingers, writhed in the gravel paths where the children used to play.
He did not know that today he would meet the thing that would end him.

The prophet came out of the marsh at the hour of long shadows.

A boy named Teodar saw him first. Teodar was nine and should not have been out by the water at all, because his mother had told him twice that week about the lights she had seen over the reeds at night, and about the Pale Hound someone had heard barking at the edge of the fen, and because the miller's girl had not come home at all.

But Teodar was nine, and a nine-year-old boy who has been warned twice is a nine-year-old boy who must go see for himself.

He was turning over a flat, black stone at the water's edge when the man stepped out of the reeds.

The reeds did not bend for him. They parted, the way a crowd parts for a king. Afterwards, when Teodar told the story a hundred times, this was always the detail he came back to. The reeds did not bend. They made way.

The man was tall and narrow and wet. His robes had once been white, Teodar thought, but now they were the color of old linen left under a bed — yellowed, mottled, thin at the knees. His beard was dark and knotted. A single braid had been plaited through it and weighted with a river stone.
Around his bare feet, in the mud, the water glowed faintly green. Teodar would remember that too.

The man looked at Teodar and smiled. It was, the boy told his grandmother later, the kindest smile he had ever seen on a face, and also the wrongest. He couldn't say how it was wrong. Only that a smile, a proper smile, has its edges in the mouth, and this one did not. It had no edges anywhere. It just went on.

"Child," the man said. His voice was soft and clear and had the faintest lisp, like a man speaking around a loose tooth. "Are you unwell?"

Teodar had not been unwell a moment before. But as the man spoke, the boy became aware of a small, hot stitch behind his left eye. A pulled muscle in his neck he had not noticed all morning. A filling of spit under his tongue, more than there should have been.

"I — I don't know, sir," Teodar said.

The man knelt down. It was a strange, slow folding, like a heron settling onto a branch. Up close he smelled of wet wood and honey that had turned.

He put two fingers under Teodar's chin and tilted the boy's face up toward the rain. The rain fell into Teodar's open mouth. It tasted sweet. Not the sweetness of sugar, but the sweetness you smell in a fruit that is one day past eating.

In the chapel, Father Aulen was trying to light the evening candles and failing. The wicks guttered. The draughts came from somewhere he could not find.

There was a knock at the heavy oak door.

He opened it and found a barefoot man in yellowed robes standing in the rain, and behind him, holding his hand, a child of about nine with a very calm, very open face and eyes that did not blink as often as they should have.

"Peace be on this house, Father," the stranger said. "The boy led me here. He said you were a good man. I have come a long way, and I am tired. May I sit by your fire?"

Father Aulen, who had buried four parishioners that week and lost his own wife to the sweating fever in the spring, looked into the stranger's face and felt, for the first time in nine months, something warm unfold in his chest.

"Come in, brother," he said. "Come in out of the rain."
He closed the door. Behind them, unnoticed, Teodar's shadow on the flagstones had two heads for a moment, and then only one.

That night, in eleven of the houses of Aldmoor, someone woke from a dream they could not remember and went to the window, and stood a long time looking at the rain.

In all eleven houses, their breath did not fog the glass.
